chapters.title: A feast of the senses
chapters.text:
Have you ever imagined what it's like to be a dog? Or how come mosquitoes are drawn to human blood? Is the world of a tick painted in the same colors as the human world, or is it black and white? And what about tastes, sounds, and other senses?

Using the term Umwelt (the German word for the environment), introduced by Estonian biologist Jakob von Uexküll, we will be trying to find answers to all these questions. Umwelt is the universe or rather a sensory bubble — how an individual experiences their world constitutes their perception of this world. Thus, each living creature experiences its surroundings differently, and we all have our Umwelten that rarely coincide. However limiting this may sound, it does not feel so to us or other species, as it is all we have ever known — a penetrating illusion that it is all there is for us to know.

Keep an open mind and learn about the world around you.

We all know the five central human senses: smell, taste, sight, touch, and hearing. But is it a universal set for all organisms on Earth? This summary will uncover fascinating facts about how animals perceive the world, and it will surely surprise you that it differs quite considerably from how we humans perceive it. Let's dive right in!

chapters.title: That smells fishy
chapters.text:
If you have a dog, this chapter might help you understand your pet. Dogs' primary sense for experiencing this world is smell. You’ve surely noticed how they love having plenty of sniffs everywhere. But how does their sense of smell work?

When we breathe in, the scent enters our nostrils, but when we breathe out, we lose that scent. The nose of a dog is built for the dog to get information from the air: it has apostrophe-shaped holes so that when they breathe out, they simultaneously breathe in. That is how dogs can stick to a smell trail.

When you take your dog for a walk, let them take their time to sniff around.

Noses are not the only receptor for the sense of smell. Ants smell through their antennae by picking up pheromones from their surroundings. Yes, pheromones, but not the ones we think of — people often misunderstand this notion as only female chemical emissions that cause sexual arousal. It is still unclear if human pheromones even exist and serve a function, as with evolution, humans have lost the vomeronasal organ that processes pheromone signals in animals.

Pheromones are chemicals that members of the same species give off to cause a social response. Ants use pheromones for various purposes, from marking trails to announcing a call for battle.

Another animal uses the tongue, the organ typically associated with taste, for smelling. Snakes' famous forked tongues are a sophisticated and fascinating tool for navigating the world. Strangely, it has no taste buds but helps snakes see the world almost in stereo. The “forks” of the tongue expand into two opposite directions and gather chemicals that guide them toward a safer path or a source of nutrition. If there is no smell of food to the left, but there is to the right, the snake will proceed to move right. If there is no smell from either side, it will continue moving forward and explore the surroundings until it finds food.

chapters.title: Keep an eye on the eyes
chapters.text:
Odorant receptors help capture molecules to distinguish smells. Another type of protein receptor, called opsins, can catch and hold the molecules that are flying past. This process lies at the basis of vision.

In terms of sight, jumping spiders have the closest resemblance to humans. Many other spiders mostly experience the world through touch, but jumping spiders rely heavily on vision. They have separate sets of eyes, some for vision and others for detecting motion.

Starfish’s eyes fit their needs perfectly. Their eyes are at the tips of their fingers. Quite literally, they have eyes on the ends of their five arms. Though they don't see color or motion, they do see what they need to — large objects to stay away from, and that's it.

The first step to understanding another animal's Umwelt is to understand what it uses its senses for. ~ Ed Yong

When it comes to seeing colors, it is vital to understand the mechanics behind how colors work. Light has a wide range of wavelengths that define color: for instance, blue has 450 to 495 nanometers, and green has 495 to 570 nanometers. Human opsins contain three cones, and each is stimulated and absorbs specific light wavelengths: long, medium, and short, also known as red, green, and blue.

Dogs and horses, in comparison, have two cones; thus, they are dichromats. Interestingly, the orange markers we paint on race obstacles are hardly visible to horses because it is challenging for them to distinguish the color orange. But bright yellow, blue, or white markers would be easy for them to spot. “Color-blind” people are also usually dichromats and, undoubtedly, should not be perceived as having a disability. But we have created a culture so reliant on a widespread trichromatic vision that it might seem they do.

Perceive unique abilities as keys to a better understanding of the world.

Did you know? In one of the Spanish provinces, 342 griffon vultures crashed into plane turbines in a span of ten years. Their vision has blind spots, and when they are circling over and looking down to spot prey, that blind spot is right above their head.

chapters.title: What a pain in the neck
chapters.text:
Pain is subjective, like colors not being perceived similarly across animal species. Nociceptors are neurons that are responsible for the feeling of pain. Their tips, located in our skin and organs, have sensors that react to various stimuli, causing sensations of pain.

The crucial duality about the nature of pain lies in the difference between two notions: pain itself and nociception. Imagine you touched a hot kettle. Your neurons contacted with high temperatures — that was nociception. Nociceptors then send signals to your brain, which reacts with discomfort and stress — pain. Although they seem similar, they are different yet inseparable; pain lasts longer than nociception, while the latter causes the former.

What about animals? Long ago, there was a belief that animals could not feel pain or emotions. With time, those views evolved; now, it is universally acknowledged that mammals feel pain. Scientists who study pain thus meet moral dilemmas when they need to hurt animals consciously to get sufficient research data.

Instead of aiming to discover the presence or absence of animal pain as we know it, it is better to find reasons why animals need to feel this sensation; it may have advantages for their adaptation to the environment, or it helps them learn something. With this different way of thinking, we can better scratch the surface of animal experience on Earth.

While we may not always understand the extent of pain animals feel, treating them with respect and compassion is essential. Many animal welfare organizations work tirelessly to ensure that animals are not subjected to unnecessary pain, whether in animal testing or inhumane treatment in the food industry. By recognizing the reality of animal pain, we can work towards creating a more just and compassionate world for all creatures, great and small.

Be mindful of your actions towards animals to avoid causing them unnecessary suffering.

chapters.title: A touching experience
chapters.text:
Through contact with stimuli, an organism can experience pain. But contact also creates a connection between the whole world and a creature who explores through touch.

Our fingers are one of the most sensitive organs of touch in nature. But the star-nosed mole might steal the show. His nose has 11 pairs of finger-like rays with many touch sensors forming an Eimer's organ — a tactile system for exploring and navigating the world. The mole gets a 3D model of its surroundings by pressing its star snout against the walls of tunnels or objects.

Like the star-nosed mole, other animals rely on touch when vision and light are unavailable. Take mallards — their beaks seem sturdy and only used for pecking, right? But in fact, duck beaks are covered in keratin, which can also be found in our fingernails, and contain many touch receptors that help ducks navigate in murky waters for food.

Whisking is another touching feat performed by animals with whiskers: mice, rats, and opossums. Each whisker sits in a cup filled with nerves and receptors that send signals to the brain. While humans move their eyes to see an object, mice do so with their whiskers, mapping the world around them. However, cats and dogs do not perform whisking, and humans and apes have sacrificed whiskers to have sensitive hands instead.

Take a moment to appreciate the sense of touch; hug your loved ones.

For some, touch can be a matter of life and death. Tiger wandering spiders have thousands of hairs on their legs, packed so dense and so sensitive that it doesn't have to create a web to feed. It just sits there and waits for its prey to come. Their primarily sensitive hairs, called trichobothria, help the spider recognize the power of the wind that lessens in the evening. He then walks out on a leaf and waits to sense the intensifying vibration of an insect flying toward him. Imagine the level of sensitivity in those hairs that catch the change in the air during a fly's flight!

chapters.title: Music to my ears
chapters.text:
For a long time, people have regarded vibrations as visual signals when, in reality, they are of high value to animals in the realm of sound and hearing. What may not be perceptible by the human ear is very much audible to treehoppers or crickets, whose main communication channel is surface vibration.

By giving in to our preconceptions, we miss what might be right in front of us. ~ Ed Yong

Owls demonstrate another example of acute hearing that helps them hunt at night. For comparison, let's recall the structure of a human ear. It has outer, middle, and inner ears. Owls' ears are built almost identically, but the outer ear is their whole face instead of flaps like we have. Their feathers and enormous ear openings behind the eyes form a unique radio location-like system, contributing to the owl's exceptional sense of hearing.

The famous bird songs also have a fascinating science background. The singing of an Australian zebra finch sounds very pleasing to the human ear. But do other finches hear the songs of their fellows in the same way as people? In terms of pitch — yes. But in terms of speed, things get interesting.

In the 1960s, Masakazu Konishi found evidence that bird hearing works at a different timescale than ours. He experimented with sparrows by playing quick sets of clicks and checking their brain activity. It turns out that a bird's brain process sounds super fast! Even when the clicks were crazy close together (1.3 to 2 milliseconds apart), the sparrows' neurons fired perfectly in sync. For comparison, a cat's ears struggle to keep up with such speedy clicks, managing to match the rhythm only about 10% of the time.

Approach new information with curiosity and avoid jumping to conclusions.

In contrast to the sense of touch, hearing can operate effectively over extended distances. In contrast to vision, hearing remains functional in complete darkness and can penetrate barriers. Unlike vibrations, hearing doesn't require a surface. Furthermore, unlike smell, hearing operates at a significantly faster speed — the speed of sound. All these features perfectly explain why some animals heavily depend on this sense.

chapters.title: The invisible senses
chapters.text:
Fish electricity existed long before humans discovered what electricity was. Approximately 350 fish species emit electromagnetic waves, and this ability enormously helped advance the study of electricity. The king of this superpower is an electric eel. One of these eel species can emit enough voltage to kill a horse.

Living in murky waters and having poor vision, electric eels found a way to deal with those problems with their electricity. It helps them locate their prey. There are three ways they use electric power:

1. Low voltage for electrolocation
2. Short and high voltage for hunting
3. The highest frequency for attacks

Magnetic fields are another earthly tool animals use to live in this world. Turtles, lobsters, and many other species have an ability called magnetoreception. It helps them navigate through misty surroundings, find their paths in deep waters, or sail through clouded skies. It is what helps them migrate. But however crucial this sense is to many creatures, it is the most understudied.

Appreciate the diversity of life on Earth and work towards preserving it.

The most prominent magnetic navigators are sea turtles. In 1991, Ken Lohmann discovered that turtles have not one but two magnetic senses. The first deals with the angle at which the Earth's magnetic field lines meet the surface — like how those lines run parallel at the equator and stand straight up at the magnetic poles. The second concerns the magnetic field's strength, which varies worldwide. It's like turtles have a built-in GPS using these magnetic coordinates, letting them read the Earth's magnetic map as they swim through the ocean.

Did you know? It is not entirely accurate when we say humans have only five senses. We have two others: proprioception, awareness of your body, and equilibrioception, the sense of balance.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
All the senses are equally important in the functioning of a living being. Animals cross-reference them, comparing and contrasting what's useful and not, and we need to follow suit to understand another creature’s experience on Earth.

Learning about animals — their senses in particular — is essential to understanding their lives rather than discovering how to use them to serve people. We need to study and analyze every feature, organ, and vibration, however minuscule they might seem to us, because they mean the world to animals.

Try this


• Go for an observation session in the nearest park: try to look at how dogs behave or what ants in the grass are doing.
• Visit an inclusive museum: experience the world as visually impaired people do.
• Look up a project by Marshmallow Laser Feast, “In the Eyes of the Animal,” where you can discover forests through the eyes of various animals.

Read it till the end?
